skill.name: scaffolding-openai-agents
skill.description: Scaffolds OpenAI Agents SDK projects with async runners, function tools, handoffs, guardrails, streaming, and FastAPI integration patterns.
skill.summary: Create structured OpenAI Agents SDK examples for multi-agent Python applications.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are false positives caused by fenced Markdown examples and benign setup text, including shell commands, placeholder API key export, hardcoded documentation URLs, weak crypto keywords, and local file existence checks. One confirmed issue remains: SKILL.md teaches an agent tool that returns eval(expression) on tool input, which can produce unsafe generated code if copied. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, obfuscation, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe eval() in agent tool example
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: SKILL.md shows a function tool that returns eval(expression) from a tool argument. The example is instructional rather than automatically executed, but copied code could allow arbitrary Python execution through agent-controlled or user-controlled input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line directly evaluates the expression argument with eval(), and the surrounding tool exposes that argument to agent use. The only mitigating context is that this appears in Markdown tutorial content and includes a brief safe_eval comment.

low_findings.title: Fenced Markdown commands flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The external command findings map to Markdown code fences and tutorial snippets. They are example setup and Python code blocks in SKILL.md, not hidden commands executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are code fence boundaries or visible instructional examples. No evidence found that the skill automatically invokes these commands.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documented example endpoints
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with low severity: SKILL.md includes URLs for wttr.in, Python documentation, and OpenAI traces. They support examples and documentation; no credential exfiltration or suspicious destination was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network indicators are real URLs, but their context is weather lookup, documentation reference, and OpenAI trace viewing. The examples do not send secrets to those URLs.

low_findings.title: API key text is a setup placeholder
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: SKILL.md line 23 instructs users to export an OPENAI_API_KEY placeholder. It does not read, collect, print, or transmit environment secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is a visible setup command using sk-... as a placeholder. No code path accesses process environment values for exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are keyword false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged locations do not contain MD5, SHA1, or cryptographic operations. They are frontmatter, handoff descriptions, tool descriptions, or verify.py string checks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of each flagged line found no cryptographic API or hash algorithm use. The static rule appears to have matched unrelated words or metadata.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance alerts are benign content checks
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: scripts/verify.py checks whether SKILL.md contains required text, and the SKILL.md lines are argument documentation or guardrail output strings. No host reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The verify script only reads the local SKILL.md file and checks expected strings. The SKILL.md locations do not query system state or enumerate host details.

low_findings.title: Critical heuristic combination not confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The combined code execution, network, and credential indicators come from separate tutorial examples. No evidence found that the skill combines these into credential theft, obfuscation, or unauthorized network transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risky patterns are visible examples in an instructional skill, not an integrated payload. Manual review found no prompt injection or exfiltration logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic evaluation of tool input
dangerous_patterns.description: The calculator tool example uses eval(expression). This pattern is dangerous in agent tools because model-selected or user-provided inputs can execute arbitrary Python if copied into a live application.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact code pattern is present at the flagged line and the argument is intended to come from tool input. The risk is reduced only because it is tutorial Markdown rather than an installed executable module.

content.value_statement: OpenAI Agents SDK projects can become inconsistent when teams mix patterns. This skill gives structured scaffolding for agents, tools, handoffs, guardrails, streaming, and FastAPI integration.
actual_capabilities: Shows OpenAI Agents SDK setup with virtual environments and API key configuration.
Demonstrates Agent class configuration with instructions, models, settings, and structured outputs.
Provides async, sync, streaming, and conversation continuation Runner patterns.
Explains function tools, typed tool inputs, agent handoffs, and agents used as tools.
Includes guardrail, shared context, FastAPI, tracing, and project structure examples.
limitations: It provides scaffolding examples, not a complete production application.
It assumes Python and the OpenAI Agents SDK are the chosen stack.
One calculator example uses eval and should be replaced before production use.
It does not cover non-SDK frameworks such as LangChain.

use_cases.title: Prototype a tutoring agent
use_cases.description: Create a Python tutoring assistant with specialist handoffs, homework guardrails, and session context.

use_cases.title: Design a multi-agent workflow
use_cases.description: Plan routing, specialist agents, shared tools, and manager-agent orchestration for a business process.

use_cases.title: Add an agent API endpoint
use_cases.description: Use the FastAPI patterns to expose agent responses and streaming output through service endpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Scaffold a simple OpenAI Agents SDK assistant in Python with async Runner.run usage and clear setup steps.

prompt_templates.title: Add typed function tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an OpenAI Agents SDK agent with safe typed function tools, input validation, and no dynamic code execution.

prompt_templates.title: Build specialist handoffs
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a triage agent that routes requests to three specialist agents using OpenAI Agents SDK handoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan production integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a production-ready FastAPI integration for an OpenAI Agents SDK workflow with streaming, tracing, context, and guardrails.

output_examples.input: Create a Python tutor agent with routing for concepts, debugging, and practice questions.
output: A triage agent design with three specialist agents.
Suggested handoff descriptions and routing instructions.
Guardrail ideas for homework-safe tutoring behavior.

output_examples.input: Add a weather tool and stream the answer through an API endpoint.
output: An async tool pattern for external data lookup.
A streaming response flow for FastAPI.
Security notes about validating user input before network calls.

output_examples.input: Review my planned agent architecture before implementation.
output: A concise architecture review covering agents, tools, guardrails, and context.
A list of missing validation and observability concerns.

best_practices: Replace dynamic evaluation examples with safe parsers or whitelisted operations.
Define narrow tool schemas and validate all user-controlled inputs before tool execution.
Add guardrails, tracing, and tests before exposing agents through an API.
anti_patterns: Do not copy the eval-based calculator example into production code.
Do not expose broad network tools without domain and input validation.
Do not mix OpenAI Agents SDK patterns with unrelated agent frameworks in the same scaffold.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps scaffold Python applications that use the OpenAI Agents SDK for agents, tools, handoffs, guardrails, and streaming.

faq.question: Does it create a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples that developers adapt into their own project structure.

faq.question: Which AI coding tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it safe for production use?
faq.answer: Use it as guidance, but review generated code. Replace unsafe dynamic evaluation and validate tool inputs before deployment.

faq.question: Does it require OpenAI API credentials?
faq.answer: Yes. The setup example expects an OpenAI API key in the environment for SDK calls.

faq.question: Does it cover LangChain or other frameworks?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on the OpenAI Agents SDK and explicitly excludes other agent frameworks.
